questions.question: Explain the Initiation Phase of Project Management Life Cycle and its key outputs.
keyPoints: Definition and purpose of Initiation Phase
Activities: concept definition, feasibility study, stakeholder identification
Key output: Project Charter
High-level goals and objectives are established

questions.question: What is Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)? Explain its importance in project planning.
keyPoints: Definition: hierarchical decomposition of project scope
Components: deliverables, work packages
Importance: defines what needs to be done, helps in estimation and responsibility assignment
WBS dictionary explains each element

questions.question: Differentiate between Project Management Life Cycle (PMLC) and Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC).
keyPoints: PMLC: manages project process (planning, tracking, resources)
SDLC: manages technical implementation (requirements to maintenance)
PMLC is overarching, SDLC is technical subset
PMLC ensures project success, SDLC ensures product quality

questions.question: Describe the Monitoring and Controlling Phase and its importance in project success.
keyPoints: Tracks project performance against baseline
Identifies variances in scope, schedule, cost
Implements corrective and preventive actions
Manages changes to project plan
Runs concurrently with Execution phase

questions.question: List and explain the key activities performed in the Closure Phase of a project.
keyPoints: Final product/service delivery
Formal acceptance from stakeholders
Documentation completion and archiving
Resource release and team reassignment
Post-project review and lessons learned
Celebration of project success

questions.question: What is scope creep? Explain any two techniques to prevent it.
keyPoints: Definition: uncontrolled expansion of project scope
Techniques: 1) Clear and documented requirements, 2) Change control board/process, 3) Define scope boundaries explicitly, 4) Regular scope verification meetings

questions.question: Explain the concept of critical path in project scheduling. Why is it important?
keyPoints: Critical path: longest sequence of dependent activities
Determines minimum project duration
Critical activities have zero float/slack
Any delay on critical path delays entire project
Important for resource allocation and schedule compression

questions.question: Discuss all five phases of the Project Management Life Cycle in detail, explaining the key activities and outputs of each phase.
keyPoints: Initiation: concept, feasibility, charter, stakeholders
Planning: PMP, WBS, schedule, budget, risk plan
Execution: deliverables creation, team management, coordination
Monitoring & Controlling: performance tracking, variance analysis, change management
Closure: delivery, acceptance, documentation, review
Explain how phases are interconnected and iterative

questions.question: What is a Project Charter? Explain its contents and significance in project management. How does it differ from a Project Management Plan?
keyPoints: Project Charter definition and purpose
Key contents: project purpose, objectives, scope, constraints, deliverables, milestones, budget, risks, stakeholders, authority
Significance: formal authorization, establishes project-manager authority
Difference: Charter is initiated early, PMP is comprehensive planning document; Charter is document, PMP is execution guide

questions.question: Explain the concept of risk management in projects. Discuss the risk management process and how risks are handled throughout the project life cycle.
keyPoints: Risk management definition and importance
Process: Risk identification, qualitative/quantitative analysis, response planning, implementation, monitoring
Risk response strategies: Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept
Risk register and its components
Risk management is ongoing throughout all phases

questions.question: Describe the role of a project manager in each phase of the Project Management Life Cycle. What skills are essential for effective project management?
keyPoints: Initiation: define project, gain authorization, identify stakeholders
Planning: create plans, define scope, estimate resources
Execution: coordinate team, manage deliverables, communicate
Monitor & Control: track progress, manage changes, ensure quality
Closure: deliver final product, obtain acceptance, release resources
Essential skills: leadership, communication, problem-solving, time management, technical knowledge
